title: The tall teenager with dislocated lenses and a clot
blurb: Marfan-like habitus but with intellectual disability, downward lens dislocation, and thrombosis — a transsulfuration defect that floods the blood with homocysteine.

patient.complaint: Blurred vision from a dislocated lens; presented with a leg DVT.

opening: 16-year-old, tall and thin with long limbs, presents with a deep vein thrombosis of the leg — and on exam has downward-and-inward lens dislocation in both eyes. He has mild intellectual disability and a fair, flushed complexion. Plasma homocysteine is very high and methionine is elevated; the urine nitroprusside test is positive. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Learning difficulties since childhood. Progressive myopia, then diagnosed lens dislocation. No prior thrombosis until now. Diet normal. Parents non-consanguineous (autosomal recessive). A maternal cousin had early stroke.
exam: Tall, arachnodactyly, pectus, joint laxity (marfanoid). Downward lens subluxation (ectopia lentis) bilaterally. Malar flush, fair skin. Mild cognitive impairment. Right calf swollen/tender (DVT). No aortic murmur today.

prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why high methionine AND high homocysteine (vs B12 deficiency), and why the thrombosis + downward lens dislocation?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the transsulfuration step that fails in classic homocystinuria.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — cystathionine β-synthase (CBS, the B6-dependent transsulfuration step). Homocysteine can't be funneled to cysteine, so homocysteine accumulates (and is remethylated back to methionine, raising it too). Excess homocysteine damages endothelium/collagen → thrombosis and lens zonule weakness (downward dislocation). Some patients respond to high-dose B6.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The block is the B6-dependent step that commits homocysteine to cysteine (transsulfuration).
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: CBS (B6) (Homocysteine → Cysteine). High methionine + high homocysteine + low cysteine = CBS deficiency; pyridoxine (B6) helps responsive forms.

mcqs.stem: How does CBS-deficiency homocystinuria differ biochemically from B12-deficiency hyperhomocysteinemia?
choices: CBS deficiency: HIGH methionine, normal MMA. B12 deficiency: LOW/normal methionine and HIGH methylmalonic acid
mcqs.explanation: CBS blocks homocysteine's exit to cysteine, so it's remethylated to methionine → methionine HIGH, MMA normal. B12 deficiency blocks methionine synthase (and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase) → methionine low/normal and MMA elevated. Methionine and MMA separate the two.

mcqs.stem: Why is the lens dislocation DOWNWARD in homocystinuria (vs upward in Marfan)?
choices: Homocysteine disrupts the zonular fibers differently; classically downward/inward in homocystinuria, upward/outward in Marfan
mcqs.explanation: Both cause ectopia lentis, but the classic teaching is DOWNWARD (and inward) in homocystinuria versus UPWARD (and outward) in Marfan syndrome — a high-yield discriminator alongside the thrombosis and intellectual disability seen in homocystinuria.

mcqs.stem: What is first-line treatment, and what is trialed first?
choices: Trial high-dose pyridoxine (B6); for non-responders, a methionine-restricted diet with cysteine, plus betaine and folate/B12
mcqs.explanation: About half of CBS patients are B6 (pyridoxine)-responsive — always trial it. Otherwise restrict methionine, supplement cysteine, and add betaine (remethylates homocysteine to methionine via an alternate route) plus folate/B12 to lower homocysteine and reduce thrombotic risk.
